title: Turkish delight
subtitle: Rose-scented starch-gel sweet dusted with icing sugar and cornflour
excerpt: Rose-flavoured Turkish delight made by cooking a concentrated sugar syrup thickened with cornflour to a firm, yielding gel. The set mixture is cut into cubes and dusted generously with a mixture of icing sugar and cornflour to prevent sticking. The result is fragrant, translucent, and tender. The process is slow and requires constant stirring but no special equipment beyond a sugar thermometer. Homemade is dramatically better than commercial.
sourceNotes: Turkish delight (lokum) is an Ottoman confection made from starch and sugar documented in Turkish cookery literature since the eighteenth century. A form of lokum appears in Mahmud Nedim bin Tosun, Ayin-i-Sofra (1880), and the preparation using cornflour as a thickening agent is consistent across late Ottoman and early twentieth-century Turkish confectionery literature. The rose-flavoured version scented with rosewater became internationally known following its introduction to Britain and Europe in the nineteenth century.

content.text: Turkish delight is mostly patient stirring. The long cooking time at low heat cooks out the raw starch taste and develops the gel. Test
when it has set enough to pour
by spooning a little onto a cold plate: it should hold its shape. Do not rush the setting time or it will be too soft to cut.

Lightly oil the square tin and dust with cornflour. In a medium saucepan, combine the caster sugar, cream of tartar, and 200ml of water. Bring to the boil over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Cook to 115°C on the sugar thermometer. Remove from heat.

In a separate bowl, whisk the cornflour with 300ml of cold water until smooth. Pour in the hot sugar syrup in a slow steady stream, whisking constantly. Return the mixture to the saucepan over low heat. Cook, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon, for 45 to 60 minutes until the mixture is thick, opaque, and pulls away from the sides of the pan.

Remove from the heat. Stir in the rosewater and food colouring if using. Pour into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Leave at room temperature for at least 6 hours or overnight until fully set and firm. Turn out onto a board dusted with the icing sugar and cornflour mixture. Cut into 2cm cubes with an oiled knife. Toss the cubes in the icing sugar and cornflour mixture to coat. Store in an airtight tin.
